title: Isaiah 59
subtitle: Your Sins Have Hidden His Face from You

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — “Your Iniquities Have Separated You from Your God”

heb.paragraph: "Surely the arm of the LORD is not too short to save, nor his ear too dull to hear. But your iniquities (ʿāwōn) have separated you from your God; your sins have hidden his face from you" (vv.1–2). The problem is not God power but human sin. Hands stained with blood, lips speak lies (v.3). Justice is far away; righteousness does not reach us (v.9). "We grope along the wall like the blind" (v.10). "Truth has stumbled in the streets; honesty cannot enter" (v.14). The catalogue of social collapse: lies, injustice, violence, blindness, stumbling truth. "The LORD looked and was displeased that there was no justice. He saw that there was no one; he was appalled that there was no one to intervene" (vv.15–16).

notes.note: "The arm of the LORD is not too short. But your iniquities have separated you." The diagnosis: not divine weakness but human sin. The arm (51:9) is strong; the barrier is moral, not mechanical.
"Justice is far away. We grope like the blind. We look for light, but all is darkness." The confession shifts to first person: WE grope, WE growl. The community owns its blindness.
"Truth has stumbled in the streets. The LORD saw that there was no one to intervene." No human intercessor available. The vacancy sets up the divine warrior of v.16–17.

refs.note: Paul compiles Isaiah's indictment with other texts to establish universal human sinfulness under the law.
The psalm's declaration that none does good parallels Isaiah's portrait of systemic corruption.
The proverb about feet rushing to shed blood appears verbatim in Isaiah's catalogue of sins.

hist.context: The catalogue of sins (lying, violence, injustice) describes post-exilic Judah's moral condition. The legal metaphors (justice, righteousness, truth) suggest covenant lawsuit language. Sin is presented not merely as individual failure but as communal darkness that blinds the nation. The image of groping like the blind at noon captures spiritual disorientation.

sections.header: Verses 16–21 — “He Put on Righteousness as a Breastplate”

heb.paragraph: "The Redeemer (gōʾēl) will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who repent of their sins" (v.20). No human intervenes, so God arms HIMSELF: righteousness as breastplate, salvation as helmet, vengeance as clothing, zeal as cloak (v.17). Paul adapts this for the believer armour in Ephesians 6:14–17. "From the west, people will fear the name of the LORD, and from the rising of the sun, they will revere his glory. For he will come like a pent-up flood that the breath of the LORD drives along" (v.19). Then the Redeemer comes to Zion (v.20). Paul quotes v.20 in Romans 11:26: "The deliverer will come from Zion." God covenant: "My Spirit, who is on you, will not depart from you, and my words that I have put in your mouth will always be on your lips, and on the lips of your children, and on the lips of their descendants, from this time on and forever" (v.21).

notes.note: "He saw that there was no one. So his own arm achieved salvation. He put on righteousness as a breastplate, salvation as a helmet." God arms himself because no human could. Ephesians 6:14–17 adapts the armour for believers.
"The Redeemer will come to Zion." Romans 11:26: "The deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob." Paul key verse for Israel future salvation comes from Isaiah 59.

"My Spirit on you will not depart, and my words in your mouth will not depart — from now and forever." The permanent covenant: Spirit and word, generation after generation. The guarantee that the prophetic word will never be silenced.

refs.note: Paul's armor of God draws directly on Isaiah's description of YHWH donning righteousness and salvation.
Paul adapts the breastplate and helmet imagery for believers awaiting the day of the Lord.
Paul quotes the Redeemer coming to Zion to confirm Israel's eventual salvation.

hist.context: When no human intercessor appears, YHWH himself arms for battle. The divine warrior imagery draws on ancient Near Eastern combat mythology but redirects it: God fights for his people when they cannot fight for themselves. The Redeemer coming to Zion addresses those who turn from transgression, maintaining the prophetic tension between judgment and hope.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVTheir feet rush into sin; they are swift to shed innocent blood. They pursue evil schemes; acts of violence mark their ways.ESVTheir feet run to evil, and they are swift to shed innocent blood; their thoughts are thoughts of iniquity; desolation and destruction are in their highways.NIVBut your iniquities have separated you from your God; your sins have hidden his face from you, so that he will not hear.ESVbut your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear.

rec.text: ""The arm of the LORD is not too short. But your iniquities have separated you." The diagnosis: not divine weakness but human sin. The arm (51:9) is strong; the barrier is moral, not mechanical."

rows.label: Sin as Barrier — 59:1–15
rows.text: "Your INIQUITIES have separated you." Not God weakness but human sin. "Truth has STUMBLED in the streets." No one to intervene.

hebtext.note: "Surely the arm of the LORD is not too short to save, nor his ear too dull to hear. But your iniquities (ʿāwōn) have separated you from your God; your sins have hidden his face from you" (vv.1–2). The problem is not God power but human sin. Hands stained with blood, lips speak lies (v.3). Justice is far away; righteousness does not reach us (v.9). "We grope along the wall like the blind" (v.10). "Truth has stumbled in the streets; honesty cannot enter" (v.14). The catalogue of social collapse: lies, injustice, violence, blindness, stumbling truth. "The LORD looked and was displeased that there was no justice. He saw that there was no one; he was appalled that there was no one to intervene" (vv.15–16).

"The Redeemer (gōʾēl) will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who repent of their sins" (v.20). No human intervenes, so God arms HIMSELF: righteousness as breastplate, salvation as helmet, vengeance as clothing, zeal as cloak (v.17). Paul adapts this for the believer armour in Ephesians 6:14–17. "From the west, people will fear the name of the LORD, and from the rising of the sun, they will revere his glory. For he will come like a pent-up flood that the breath of the LORD drives along" (v.19). Then the Redeemer comes to Zion (v.20). Paul quotes v.20 in Romans 11:26: "The deliverer will come from Zion." God covenant: "My Spirit, who is on you, will not depart from you, and my words that I have put in your mouth will always be on your lips, and on the lips of your children, and on the lips of their descendants, from this time on and forever" (v.21).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 9: the divine warrior (Eph 6:14-17), the Redeemer coming to Zion (Rom 11:26), the Spirit-and-word covenant forever. Justice and Judgment: sin as barrier, truth stumbled, no one to intervene — so God acts alone.
